data.title: Fetterman calls on Trump to fire Noem: 'Americans have died'
data.description:
Centrist Democratic Sen. John Fetterman (Pa.) called on President Trump Tuesday to fire Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem after the fatal shooting of a second protester in Minneapolis over the weekend.

“President Trump: I make a direct appeal to immediately fire Secretary Noem. Americans have died. She is betraying DHS’s core mission and trashing your border security legacy,” Fetterman said in a statement.

He warned that Trump is at risk of repeating the mistake of former President Biden, who stood by his own embattled Homeland Security secretary, Alejandro Mayorkas, despite many complaints from Republicans over his handling of the job.

“DO NOT make the mistake President Biden made for not firing a grossly incompetent DHS Secretary,” the Pennsylvania Democrat said.

Fetterman on Monday called for federal officials to halt the immigration enforcement operation in Minneapolis known as Operation Metro Surge but he says he won’t risk a partial government shutdown at the end of the week by voting to block funding for the Department of Homeland Security.

“Ms. Good and Mr. Pretti should still be alive. My family grieves for theirs,” the senator said, referring to the two protesters who were shot and killed by federal officers.

“The operation in Minneapolis should stand down and immediately end. It has become an ungovernable and dangerous urban theatre for civilians and law enforcement that is incompatible with the American spirit,” he added.

His comments come as other Democrats have called for Noem’s impeachment or resignation after she called Alex Pretti, the ICU nurse shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday, a “domestic terrorist,” despite a lack of evidence to support the claim.

Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) is one Democrat calling for the DHS chief to be impeached.

 “She lied about a citizen to justify his murder. And that is not leadership. The president should ask for her resignation. But if not, I do think the House should impeach,” Gallego said in an interview with MS NOW.

Trump and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt have distanced themselves from Noem’s rhetoric.

Asked Monday if the president agreed with Noem’s characterization of the shooting incident, Leavitt told reporters: “I have not heard the president characterize Mr. Pretti in that way.”

Noem is scheduled to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee on March. 3.

Sen. Pete Welch (D-Vt.), a member of the Judiciary Committee, on Monday called Noem “a real problem.”

“Noem’s got to go. I mean, we have the head of this department who spends a lot of her time in self-promotion, the $200 million video shoot in Mount Rushmore,” Welch said in an MS NOW interview. “And then when there is a serious, serious situation involving the death of two [Minnesota] citizens, she calls them domestic terrorists.

data.title: Republicans appeal ruling throwing out only GOP-controlled NYC House seat
data.description:
Republicans are appealing a ruling that throws out Rep. Nicole Malliotakis’s (R) seat in New York and orders the creation of a new House map ahead of November.

Attorneys representing several members of the New York State Board of Elections, in addition to Malliotakis and a group of voters, appealed the ruling on Monday. Republicans are appealing the decision in both the New York State Court of Appeals and with the appellate division of the New York State Supreme Court.

Attorneys for Malliotakis and the group of voters told the Court of Appeals in a letter on Monday that Republicans also planned to file motions with both courts requesting a pause on the New York State Supreme Court’s decision to institute a new map in the state.

“Time is of the essence in this matter, as the Order has now thrown New York’s 2026 Congressional Election — slated to begin on February 24, 2026 — into chaos,” Bennet Moskowitz, the attorney for the House Republican and group of voters, wrote.

Moskowitz asked the courts to make a decision by Feb. 10 on whether the courts would pause the ruling and allow the current New York House map to be used for the 2026 midterms. If they didn’t rule by then, Moskowitz said they would take it up immediately to the U.S. Supreme Court.

Acting New York state Supreme Court Justice Jeffrey Pearlman, an appointee of Gov. Kathy Hochul (D), ruled earlier this month that New York’s 11th Congressional District, which includes Staten Island and some of southern Brooklyn, was unconstitutionally configured. He ordered for the congressional lines to be tossed out and a new House map made to be used in time for the 2026 midterms.

Malliotakis’s district went for Trump by over 24 points in 2024, and a new map could offer Democrats another pickup opportunity ahead of the November elections.

The court decision has already created uncertainty for nearby House districts, which are likely to see their congressional lines redrawn should a new map be implemented.

The decision could impact candidate filing deadlines and the timing of the state’s congressional primaries — an issue that the Empire State contended with once before during the 2022 midterms after a judge struck down a Democratic-favored map and ordered a court-appointed special master to create one instead.

data.title: Natalie Portman: Trump, Noem, ICE actions 'outrageous'
data.description:
Natalie Portman is calling out President Trump and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem following the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a federal law enforcement agent in Minnesota, saying what’s happening in the United States amid the administration’s deportation efforts is “obscene.”

“I’m getting emotional because it’s really been — it’s such a horrible day. It’s been a horrible week,” the “Black Swan” star, appearing to tear up, said during a Saturday interview ahead of the premiere of “The Gallerist” at the Sundance Film Festival in Utah.

“It’s very joyful to be at a film festival and in a creative community celebrating film together, but what is happening in our country is just obscene,” Portman said.

“What Trump, and Kristi Noem and ICE [Immigration and Customs Enforcement] are doing to our citizens, and undocumented people, is outrageous and needs to end,” the Academy Award winner said.

The 44-year-old actor’s comments followed the shooting death earlier in the day of Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse, by a Customs and Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis. Pretti’s death came weeks after an ICE officer shot and killed a 37-year-old woman, Renee Good, during the Department of Homeland Security’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota. 

Trump said Sunday that his administration was “reviewing everything and will come out with a determination” regarding Pretti’s death.

“I don’t like any shooting. I don’t like it,” Trump said in an interview with The Wall Street Journal.

Portman told reporters at the red carpet event that she was “very moved” by “all of the people coming out and supporting each other, community, and showing the best of America — really being incredible neighbors and citizens.”

“But it’s just too much,” she said. “It needs to end.”

data.title: Live updates: Senate returns amid DHS funding uproar as shutdown deadline looms
data.description:
The Senate heads back to Washington on Tuesday as a partial government shutdown looms. But Democrats are eyeing funding for Department of Homeland Security (DHS), after the weekend killing of a Minneapolis man by a Border Patrol (CBP) agent.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has indicated he intends to move forward with a six-bill funding package, which includes DHS, despite Democrats’ threats to block the measure. Thune must confront whether he can rally his conference and the White House around a modified bill that separates DHS funding from the package to avoid another shutdown.

It also comes as President Trump is making adjustments to his signature initiatives in the wake of Alex Pretti’s death on Saturday. Border czar Tom Homan will arrive Tuesday in Minneapolis, replacing Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino.

The shooting ignited a firestorm on both sides of the aisle, with Democrats calling for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to be fired or impeached, and Republican lawmakers expressing grave concern and calling for hearings.

Trump on Tuesday will head to Des Moines, Iowa, to deliver remarks.

Follow live below.

data.title: The facts are in: Trump's return-to-office mandate backfired
data.description:
On Jan. 20, 2025, a signed return to in-person work memo landed on agency desks and sent managers scrambling. At the Social Security Administration, routine telework collapsed within weeks, even as phone lines stayed jammed. 

A fresh watchdog report shows the human cost: The very flexibility that drew talent in now pushes talent out. The report comes from the Government Accountability Office, and it focuses on how the decree rippled through hiring, training and retention. 

Social Security already carries a heavy service load, so every resignation shows up in longer waits and slower decisions for the public. The report’s findings, paired with broader federal research, point to a simple lesson: telework works best when leaders manage it, measure it and use it to keep skills in house. 

Government Accountability Office investigators tracked telework at the Social Security Administration from July 2019 through May 2025 and found a sharp cliff after the White House memo. Telework hours fell from 35 percent of total hours in January through March 2025 to 13 percent in April through May 2025, a drop that matched the new posture. That speed matters, because SSA employees had built their lives and budgets around flexibility.  

Agency leaders told investigators that telework acted as a recruitment lever during a tight labor market. In a fiscal 2023 new-hire survey, more than half of new employees said telework ranked as a very important factor in applying and accepting the job. Managers also described candidates who expected hybrid schedules as a baseline benefit, especially in high traffic metro areas.

Retention signals flashed even before Trump’s decree. About 37 percent of Social Security Administration respondents to the 2024 employee viewpoint survey planned to leave within a year. Among those planning an exit, almost half said telework or remote options in their unit shaped that decision. Frontline staff singled out newer hires and retirement-eligible experts as the most ready to move, since both groups value lighter commutes and focused work time.

The Government Accountability Office warned about skills gaps in mission critical roles just as the Social Security Administration announced a 50,000-employee target for in-person work on Feb. 28, 2025, in an agency workforce plan aimed at cutting costs. 

Federal law already treats telework as a management tool and a continuity asset through the Telework Enhancement Act that requires policies, training and reporting. That framework exists because agencies use telework to keep services running during weather events, security incidents and public health disruptions.

The Office of Personnel Management’s fiscal 2023 telework status report describes agencies that used hybrid schedules as lifting recruitment and retention while sustaining performance. In the same report, Treasury’s Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau credits telework with shrinking its footprint in Washington by 22 percent and saving nearly $1 million in facilities costs, a concrete real estate savings example that private employers envy. EPA reports about $7 million a year in avoided transit subsidy spending thanks to current telework levels — another budget relief that frees up funds for mission work. 

Those dollars connect directly to retention because agencies reinvest savings in better tools, training and workspace design. When leaders pair clear performance standards with predictable flexibility, they win a recruiting edge and keep accountability high. That formula matters more in government than in most sectors because hiring cycles run long, and specialized knowledge takes years to build. 

Long before the pandemic, the Merit Systems Protection Board laid out a business case: with solid supervision, telework delivers benefits while maintaining productivity, as detailed in its telework evidence. The board also highlights continuity of operations, reduced real estate pressure, and better work-life balance from shorter commutes. 

In a 2024 performance evaluation report, the Government Accountability Office urged agencies to measure telework impacts on customer service, training and organizational health. In its new study of Social Security, the watchdog agency repeats that call and presses the agency to update its human capital plan and keep evaluating telework where offices still use it. 

Trump’s administration framed the return push around supervision and fairness, echoing language from the Jan. 22, 2025 guidance memo. But the findings reveal a hidden trade-off: forcing the same schedule on every job drains the very talent that the public relies on for timely benefits decisions. A smarter approach uses job-based eligibility, transparent metrics and targeted onsite time for training, mentoring and complex customer work. Agencies that build that system keep their best people longer, save money and deliver service with steadier staffing. 

Return mandates feel decisive, yet the audit trail shows decisive moves can create staffing turbulence. Employees who joined for flexibility start scanning the market for something better. Managers lose years of expertise with every departure. 

Government has the tools to do better: law, reporting and research tying telework to continuity and cost control. Leaders can honor in-person collaboration by designing office time around work needs, using data to adjust. When the federal workforce stays whole, the public feels it first. 

Gleb Tsipursky, Ph.D., serves as CEO of the hybrid work consultancy Disaster Avoidance Experts and authored the best-seller “Returning to the Office and Leading Hybrid and Remote Teams.”

data.title: Minneapolis mayor says Trump 'agreed the present situation can't continue'
data.description:
Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) said President Trump acknowledged during a call Monday that “the present situation” in the city cannot continue, amid public backlash following two fatal shootings involving federal immigration officers this month.

“I spoke with President Trump today and appreciated the conversation. I expressed how much Minneapolis has benefited from our immigrant communities and was clear that my main ask is that Operation Metro Surge needs to end,” Frey wrote on social platform X, referring to the Trump administration’s ramped up immigration operation in Minnesota. 

“The president agreed the present situation can’t continue,” he added.

The mayor also wrote that some federal agents will begin leaving the Twin Cities on Tuesday and he “will continue pushing for the rest involved in this operation to go.” Border Patrol commander-at-large Greg Bovino is reportedly departing Minnesota Tuesday.

The president also announced Monday that he is sending border czar Tom Homan to the state to oversee immigration enforcement. Frey said he will meet with Homan on Tuesday.

Trump wrote on Truth Social after his conversation with Frey that “lots of progress is being made!”

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) surged Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Border Patrol personnel to Minnesota earlier this month, amid a sweeping federal probe into alleged fraud in the state’s social services programs. 

The North Star State has the largest Somali community of any state in the U.S., with an estimated 107,000 individuals of Somali descent living in the state in 2024, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Days after the initial surge, 37-year-old Renee Good was fatally shot in her car by an ICE officer. On Saturday, 37-year-old Alex Pretti, an ICU nurse was shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent. 

Their deaths sparked demonstrations against the operations and calls from local and state officials, including Frey and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D), for ICE and Border Patrol to leave.

Walz and Trump also spoke via phone on Monday. The governor wrote on X that the president “agreed to look into reducing the number of federal agents in Minnesota.” 

Trump, meanwhile, wrote on Truth Social that the two “seemed to be on a similar wavelength.”

“I told Governor Walz that I would have Tom Homan call him, and that what we are looking for are any and all Criminals that they have in their possession,” he added. “The Governor, very respectfully, understood that, and I will be speaking to him in the near future.”

data.title: There's no way to normalize Trump's attacks on democracy
data.description:
Last Tuesday was the one-year anniversary of Trump 2.0. And let’s look at the sorry record. 

Among other things, a drip feed disclosure of Epstein files, ordered to be produced on Nov. 19, we are told there are 5.2 million pages to go. Extrajudicial killings on the high seas, outsized assertions of executive power, resignations of U.S. attorneys over questionable prosecutions, threats to annex Greenland because Trump is miffed that Norway denied him the Nobel Peace Prize. And hovering over it all investigations and prosecutions of political enemies. 

In November, Sen. Elissa Slotkin (D-Mich.) and five other Democratic members of Congress with backgrounds in military or intelligence service made a video directly quoting the Uniform Code of Military Justice about the military’s responsibility to reject illegal orders.

Their message came straight out of the 1945-46 Nuremberg trials of 22 Nazi leaders accused, among other things, of “crimes against humanity” where the international court ruled that the “I was only following orders” defense would not wash. 

The video did not mention any specific orders, but the six were plainly referring to the sinking of Venezuelan ships in international waters topped off by the killing of survivors clinging to the wreckage of their vessel.  The strikes on the suspected drug-trafficking boats had no apparent legal basis

Trump blasted the video, calling it “SEDITIOUS BEHAVIOR, punishable by DEATH!” He added: “Each one of these traitors to our Country should be ARRESTED AND PUT ON TRIAL.” Imagine! Legislators who disagree with Trump executed by lethal injection.

Trump all too often displays an unseemly lust for the arrest, prosecution, trial and even the possible execution of his political enemies — notably former FBI director James Comey, New York Attorney General Letitia James, Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.), Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D). Then, topping it all off, the perennial piñata Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell for the crime of doing his duty, holding the course on interest rates.  

Slotkin reported that earlier this month, former Fox News host Jeanine Pirro, U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, asked to interview her. This follows in the footsteps of Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth, another former Fox News host, who announced he was taking administrative action against Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.), the senior legislator participating in the video.  

Kelly, a retired Navy captain and astronaut serving his first full term in the Senate, who is also a 2028 presidential hopeful, responded with a lawsuit in which he asked a federal judge to block the Pentagon’s action as “unlawful and unconstitutional.” 

“The First Amendment forbids the government and its officials from punishing disfavored expression or retaliating against protected speech,” the complaint stated. “That prohibition applies with particular force to legislators speaking on matters of public policy.” The lawsuit also argued that Kelly’s public statements about policy and personnel were lawful under Article I, Section 6 of the Constitution known as the “speech or debate” clause. 

Politicized prosecutions have no place in America; yet they are too much with us. Weaponized prosecutions undermine confidence in the criminal justice system and only lend succor to the cynic. Using prosecution to silence critics is a tactic common to authoritarian regimes. 

Trump recently summoned a group of United States attorneys to the White House to berate the lawyers, calling them “weak” and complaining they weren’t moving fast enough to prosecute his favorite political targets.  

But how does he explain even-handed justice in his pardon of the former president of Honduras, Juan Orlando Hernández, who had been sentenced to 45 years in prison on serious drug charges? Asked to explain the Hernández pardon, Trump said absurdly, “The man that I pardoned, was … treated like the Biden administration treated a man named Trump,” adding, “This was a man who was persecuted very unfairly. He was the head of the country.”  

And helicoptering over it all, we have the politicized investigation of the Immigration and Customs Enforcement shooting in Minneapolis of Renée Good, killed in her confrontation with ICE officers, which has not led to an investigation of the officer who was the shooter, but an FBI inquiry into Good’s widow. Attorney General Todd Blanche said that “there is currently no basis for a criminal civil rights investigation” into the ICE agent. How about the videotape evidence showing that Good was veering her car away from the ICE agent?  

 And then there is the most recent Minneapolis murder of Alex Pretti, the ICU nurse in the VA Hospital shot multiple times by federal officers looking more like thugs than law enforcement. The latest outrage has sparked protests across the country.  

Lawmakers, judges and other public servants, all under groundless presidential attack. Trump doesn’t even need the pretextual glow of a Reichstag fire. 

We have reached a new malignant normal in the administration of justice. As a former federal prosecutor in the Southern District of New York who lived through the Watergate era, Nixon’s shenanigans appear as normal as apple pie.  

James D. Zirin is a former federal prosecutor in the Southern District of New York and a published legal analyst.

data.title: Gallego: Noem labeling Pretti as a violent terrorist is 'impeachable'
data.description:
Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) said Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem committed an “impeachable” offense by calling Alex Pretti, the 37-year-old nurse who was shot and killed by a Border Patrol officer, a “domestic terrorist.”

“I think it’s ridiculous that someone called, you know, this poor victim a terrorist, a violent terrorist. You know, that right there is impeachable,” Gallego told MS NOW’s Katy Tur on Monday.

“The fact that she didn’t wait to see what the video that was coming out, automatically said that this man was brandishing a weapon, which we now know is not true,” he said.

 “She lied about a citizen to justify his murder. And that is not leadership. The president should ask for her resignation. But if not, I do think the House should impeach,” he added.

The Republican-controlled House is highly unlikely to move forward with articles of impeachment against Noem, but Democrats might do so in 2027 if they capture the lower chamber in the November election. 

Noem claimed without evidence that Pretti had brandished a pistol and wanted to “inflict maximum damage on individuals and to kill law enforcement,” even though video footage showed that he never drew the weapon, for which he had a permit to carry concealed.

President Trump and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt have since distanced themselves from Noem’s rhetoric.

Asked Monday if the president agreed with Noem’s characterization of the shooting incident, Leavitt told reporters: “I have not heard the president characterize Mr. Pretti in that way.”

“However, I have heard the president say he wants to let the facts in the investigation lead itself,” she said.

Separately, Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa) announced on Monday that Noem will testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee on March 3, something that the Republican chairman and Senate Democrats have asked Noem to do for months.

data.title: Bovino expected to leave Minneapolis Tuesday: Reports
data.description:
Greg Bovino, commander-at-large of the U.S. Border Patrol, is expected to depart Minnesota on Tuesday along with some of his agents, according to multiple reports.

His departure comes in the wake of the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis on Saturday. Bovino told CNN on Sunday that Pretti was attempting to “massacre” law enforcement, but video of the incident shows the ICU nurse filming agents and attempting to assist a woman they had pushed to the ground before he was surrounded, disarmed and shot.

On Monday, President Trump sent border czar Tom Homan to the North Star State, as the administration faces backlash from both sides of the aisle over the recent deaths of Pretti and Renee Good. The president wrote on Truth Social that Homan will report directly to him.

White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt wrote on social platform X that Homan will be managing Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations on the ground in Minnesota “to continue arresting the worst of the worst criminal illegal aliens.”

Leavitt also said the border czar will “coordinate” with those leading the federal probe into fraud within the state’s social services programs. 

Also Monday, Trump spoke with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey. Both Democratic officials have previously called on the president to remove ICE and Border Patrol personnel from the state.

Walz wrote on X that his conversation with the president was “productive.” He also said that he asked Trump to reduce the number of federal agents in the state.

Frey wrote on X that he reiterated his request for the operation to end and the president agreed that “the present situation can’t continue.” The mayor also said some federal agents will begin leaving the area Tuesday and he will meet with Homan.

Amid the public uproar over the recent shootings, the White House has sought to distance Trump from rhetoric shared by others in his administration. Many critics have called on Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to step down in the wake of the incidents and requested hearings into the Trump administration’s immigration tactics.

Democratic lawmakers are also threatening funding to DHS as a partial government shutdown deadline looms at the end of the week.

data.title: Democrat or Republican, Americans want their National Forests kept intact
data.description:
Twenty-five years ago, the Forest Service adopted one of the boldest conservation measures in American history, the Roadless Area Conservation Rule.  

The National Forest System already contained 380,000 miles of roads, enough to wrap around the Earth about 15 times. Carving more roads into the remaining undisturbed forests didn’t make economic or ecological sense. The Roadless Area Conservation Rule would protect more than 58 million acres of National Forests from roadbuilding and logging, across 38 states and Puerto Rico.  

The Forest Service didn’t come to this decision alone. In fact, it facilitated the most extensive public involvement process in the history of public land management. They learned that the American public wanted protections for these backcountry forestlands, too. 

Together, we have served as U.S. Forest Service chiefs for both Republican and Democratic administrations. We know that forest management decisions never come without debate, opinions and — more often than not — disagreement. Each of us has had to strike the difficult balance between leaving some forests intact, while sustainably using others to benefit communities and economies.

But regardless of who is in the White House, one thing has always remained true: Americans value their national forests, and they want to see them protected for the benefits they provide us, like clean air, water, abundant recreation opportunities and sustainable economies. 

Last summer, when the Trump administration announced its plan to repeal the Roadless Area Conservation Rule in its entirety, more than 600,000 Americans from all corners of the country spoke out, the vast majority of which opposed the rollback. We weren’t surprised. Americans cherish their public lands and they’re never silent when the places they love are under attack. 

Roadless areas are havens for wildlife, keeping habitats intact and free of industrial development. These backcountry forests protect the homes of threatened species such as California condors, grizzly bears and wolves in the Yellowstone area, native trout in the Pacific Northwest, migratory songbirds in the Appalachian hardwoods and more. They also sustain wild salmon, especially in Alaska where they are the lifeblood for both the fishing industry and traditional subsistence practices of Indigenous communities.   

The U.S. National Forests are the headwaters of our great rivers and the largest source of municipal water supply in the nation, serving over 60 million people in 3,400 communities in 33 states. The 2001 Roadless Rule is vital for maintaining clean drinking water for communities across the country. 

Major U.S. cities including Los Angeles, Portland, Denver and Atlanta receive a significant portion of their water supply from national forests. But building more roads means more water pollution, threatening these crucial water sources. These remote pristine areas provide quality dispersed recreation opportunities and are treasured for hunting, fishing, and wildlife viewing.  

Let’s not forget the reasons these backcountry areas do not have roads. The easily accessible high value timber from national forests has been harvested. What little that remains in these roadless backcountry areas is difficult and costly to access. The steep slopes, impacts to watersheds and lack of economical timber harvest and high cost of road building prevented these areas from even being considered for a road.  

Today’s National Forests don’t face the same challenges they did in 2001. But clearcutting old-growth in backcountry forests has never been a science-supported solution to the increasing threat of mega-fires across our National Forest System. 

Designated roadless areas are not a barrier to fire restoration work. In fact, the rule allows construction of roads to fight wildfires in urgent situations, and it allows forest restoration projects to clear away wildfire fuel. Our public national forests already have twice as many miles of roads as the entire U.S. national highway system. We don’t need more roads or industrial logging to deal with wildfires. 

When the Roadless Rule was proposed, the Forest Service was carrying a maintenance backlog of more than $8 billion on its existing road system. Circumstances have not improved since then. The overall deferred maintenance backlog for the agency today is $8.6 billion, and with recent staffing and budget cuts, the situation will get worse. 

Unmaintained roads lead to soil erosion, landslides in mountainous terrain and degraded water quality. Why build more roads when the agency, strapped for resources, can’t even maintain the ones that already exist?  

Twenty-five years ago, Americans spoke out in favor of protecting America’s natural wonders — pristine forests, wild rivers and iconic wildlife. That support was unwavering this summer and will once again be crystal clear in the upcoming comment period. 

The current administration needs to know: Repealing the Roadless Area Conservation Rule would not be in the long-term interest of the American people, the National Forests or the communities they serve.  

Each of us, serving as Forest Service chiefs, has been faced with tough decisions. Keeping backcountry and healthy forests free of unnecessary roads was never one of them. 

Mike Dombeck served as U.S. Forest Service chief from 1997 to 2001. Dale Bosworth served as U.S. Forest Service chief from 2001-2007. Tom Tidwell served as U.S. Forest Service chief from 2009 to 2017 and Vicki Christiansen served as U.S. Forest Service chief from 2018 to 2021.
